25: vaani yadati goa'li chai ve'acharon al afar yaqum: 'For I know that my Redeemer [go'el] lives, and at the last he will stand upon the earth' (or 'upon the dust'). This is one of the OT's most disputed verses — the text is difficult (the next verse, 19:26, is even more so: 'after my skin has thus been destroyed, yet in my flesh I shall see God'). The term go'el (kinsman-redeemer) in the context of Job's suffering suggests: the one who will vindicate Job after death, who will see to it that justice is done. Whether Job envisions a bodily resurrection (as 19:26 suggests in the MT) or a post-mortem vindication, the theological content is the same: a personal Redeemer who is alive, who will act at the last, who will secure Job's vindication. The NT identifies this Redeemer as Christ (1 Cor 15:20: Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep).
1: wayyaʿan ʾîyôḇ wayyōʾmar: 'Then Job answered and said.' The standard dialogue-opening formula appears 28 times in the book; its repetition creates the rhythm of a formal disputation. Job's response here opens his most extended and passionate speech, moving from accusation of the friends to the transcendent confession of vv.23-27.
2: ʿaḏ-ʾānāh tᵉḏaʾᵃḇûn nap̄šî ûṯᵉdakkᵉʾûn ḇiḏḇārîm: 'How long will you torment my soul and crush me with words?' Dāʾaḵ (crush, be dimmed/faint) is used of a lamp going out (Isa 42:3; 43:17) and of the spirits sinking. Dākāʾ is more violent — to shatter, reduce to powder (Ps 90:3; 94:5). The pairing: they torment his living inner self (nepeš) and simultaneously destroy him with verbal force. The cruelty the friends inflict with language is presented as physical-grade violence against Job's person.
3: zeh ʿeśer pᵉʿāmîm takᵉlîmûnî: 'Ten times now you have reproached me.' Ten times is likely idiomatic for 'repeatedly' (cf. Gen 31:7; Num 14:22) rather than a precise count of speech rounds. Kālaṃ (to humiliate, put to shame) describes public dishonor — the social violence of stripping someone of their standing before witnesses. Lōʾ-ṯēḇōšû takkᵉrû-lî: 'you are not ashamed to wrong me' — the lack of shame compounds the offense; they have no moral brake on their verbal attacks.
4: wᵉʾap̄ ʾim-šāgîṯî ʾittî tālîn mešûḡāṯî: 'Even if I have truly erred, my error remains with me.' Šāgāh = to go astray, err inadvertently (Lev 4:13; Num 15:28; Prov 19:27). The condition is hypothetical ('even if'); Job does not concede the point. The verb's semantic range emphasizes unintentional sin rather than deliberate transgression — even at its most modest, his admission would be only of an error, not of the guilt Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar are asserting. Ittî tālîn: 'it lodges with me' — the error, if any, remains in his own sphere; it does not constitute grounds for the friends' attack.
5: ʾim-ʾāmnām ʿālāy taḡdîlû wᵉṯôkîḥû ʿālay ḥerpātî: 'If you truly exalt yourselves over me and press my disgrace against me as evidence.' Gādal ʿālay (exalt yourselves over me) is the posture of domination and condescension. Tôkîaḥ (from yākaḥ = to argue/reprove/adjudicate) — their proof-from-disgrace reasoning: taking Job's suffering as evidence of guilt is a category error that the entire book exists to correct. The friends' logic is the proto-retribution theology Job is dismantling.
6: dᵉʿû ʾēp̄ô kî-ʾĕlôah ʿiwwᵉṯanî ûmṣûḏô ʿālay hiqqîp̄: 'Know then that God has wronged me (ʿiwwᵉṯanî) and thrown his net around me.' ʿĀwat (to bend, pervert, twist) is a legal term for injustice and distortion of judgment (Lam 3:36; Amos 8:5). Job dares to say that what God has done to him is, by the normal standard of justice, perversion. Mᵉṣûḏāh (net/siege-works) from ṣuḏ (to hunt); the hunting-net image opens the extended military metaphor that runs through vv.6-12. God as hunter and besieger rather than protector.
7: hēn ʾeṣʿaq ḥāmās wᵉlōʾ ʾeʿāneh: 'I shout “Violence!” (ḥāmās) but no one answers.' Ḥāmās is one of the weightiest Hebrew words for wrongful harm — the word used of the violence that provoked the flood (Gen 6:11; 6:13), the blood of Abel (Hab 2:8), and prophetic indictments throughout (Amos 3:10; Ezek 7:11). By crying ḥāmās, Job invokes the full legal-moral weight of injustice; it is the victim's cry when the offense rises to God's attention. The silence from the heavens — no answer, no verdict — is the theological crisis at the core of the book.
8: ʾorḥî gāḏar wᵉlōʾ ʾeʿĕbōr wᵉʿal nᵉṯîḇôṯay ḥōšeḵ yāśîm: 'He has blocked my way so I cannot pass; he has put darkness over my paths.' The imagery of blocked paths and enforced darkness frames Job's situation as imprisonment by divine agency. Gāḏar (to wall up, block) is used in Lam 3:7 ('he has walled me in so I cannot escape') and Hos 2:6 ('I will hedge up her way with thorns'). The blocked path and darkened way together invert the protective guidance language of Ps 23:3 ('he leads me in right paths') and Ps 119:105 ('your word is a lamp to my feet').
9: kᵉḇôḏî mēʿālay hip̄šîṭ wᵉʿăṭeret rōʾšî lāqāḥ: 'He has stripped me of my honor (kāḇôḏ) and taken the crown (ʿăṭāret) from my head.' Kāḇôḏ (honor/glory/weight) is the fundamental social-moral currency in the OT; its stripping is public humiliation at the most profound level. ʿĂṭāret rōʾš (the crown of the head) appears as a symbol of status and dignity (Prov 4:9; 12:4; 17:6). The two images together — robes stripped and crown removed — are the language of dethronement and disgrace: Job's former eminence (ch. 29) has been publicly annulled by God's action.
10: yittᵉṣēnî missābîḇ wāʾēlek wayyasēaʿ kāʾēṣ tiqwāṯî: 'He has broken me down on every side and I am gone; he has pulled up my hope like a tree.' Nāsaʿ is the verb used for uprooting tent pegs and breaking camp (Exod 12:37; 17:1; Num 9:17) — the nomadic image of a total displacement. Tiqwāh (hope, expectation — literally 'that toward which one looks forward') is a key wisdom term. Job's hope is not merely diminished but uprooted, its roots torn out — the image makes clear this is not discouragement but ontological devastation.
11: wayyaḥar ʿālay ʾappô wayyaḥšᵉḇēnî lô kᵉṣārāyw: 'He has kindled his wrath against me and counted me among his enemies.' Ḥārāh ʾap̄ (his anger burns) is the standard expression for divine wrath in action (Exod 4:14; Num 11:10; 32:13). To be counted among God's ṣārîm (adversaries, enemies, those who press against) is the most extreme possible statement about Job's condition — he is not merely abandoned but positionally assigned to the category of God's enemies. The theological shock is that Job knows himself innocent of the conduct that would deserve enemy-status.
12: yaḥad yāḇōʾû gᵉḏûḏāyw wayyiśpᵉlû ʿālay darkām wayyaḥănû sāḇîḇ lᵉʾohᵃlî: 'His troops advance together; they heap up siege ramps against their way against me and encamp around my tent.' Gᵉḏûḏ (marauding troop, raiding party) — a military force. Sālaḥ derek (heap up siege ramp) is the technical vocabulary of ancient siege warfare (cf. 2 Sam 20:15; Isa 37:33). The military metaphor is extended: God is a general conducting a systematic siege of a single individual. Job's tent (the last remnant of his domestic life) is surrounded. The image is grotesque in proportion — infinite divine power mobilized against one helpless man.
13: ʾaḥay mēʿālay hirḥîq wayyōdᵉʿay ʾak-zārû mimmennî: 'He has put my brothers far from me; those who knew me have become wholly estranged.' The catalogue of abandoned relationships in vv.13-19 is a carefully structured social inventory working inward: brothers → acquaintances → relatives → familiar friends → household guests → servants → wife → children's family → companions. Each relationship, which in ANE culture carried obligation of loyalty, has been severed. Zûr (estranged, foreign, a stranger) — those who knew him now experience him as an alien, not as a person they recognize.
14: ḥāḏᵉlû qᵉrôbāy ûmᵉyuddāʿay šᵉḵēḥûnî: 'My relatives have failed me, and my familiar friends have forgotten me.' Ḥāḏal (to cease, desist, fail) — they have simply stopped; the relational obligations they once performed have been discontinued. Šāḵaḥ (to forget) — the forgetting that in Israel's theology was the deepest relational failure (God 'forgetting' Israel in Isa 49:14; Job 'forgetting' God in 8:13). The friends have done what the faithless do: treated covenant relationships as optional when they become costly.
15: gārê ḇêṯî wᵉʾamᵉhōṯay lᵉzār taḥšᵉḇûnî nāḵrî hāyîṯî bᵉʿênêhem: 'Those in my house and my female servants count me as a stranger; I have become a foreigner in their eyes.' Zār (stranger, alien, outsider) and nāḵrî (foreign, from another nation) are the strongest words for social exclusion — Job is now 'a foreigner' in his own household. This inverts the social world of ch. 29-31: the master of a household whose servants honored him is now unrecognized in his own house. The reversal is complete when even those who share physical space treat him as an intruder.
16: lᵉʿaḇdî qārāʾtî wᵉlōʾ yaʿăneh bᵉp̄î ʾeṯḥannen-lô: 'I called my servant, but he gave me no answer; I had to beg him with my own mouth.' The collapse of the master-servant hierarchy: the servant who would previously have come at a word now does not answer, and the master must beg — literally supplicate (ḥānan = to implore, entreat favor). Ḥānan is the word used for prayer before God; here it is degraded to a plea to an ignoring slave. The reversal demonstrates that Job's former social power has been entirely stripped.
17: rûḥî zārāh lᵉʾišṯî wᵉḥannōṯî liḇnê ḇiṭnî: 'My breath is repulsive to my wife; I am loathsome to the children of my own body.' Rûaḥ (breath/spirit) — the intimacy that breath represents makes the repulsion more visceral: the most intimate physical marker of presence (breath) is the exact thing his wife finds repellent. Ḥānan liḇnê ḇiṭnî: 'imploring the sons of my womb' — beṭen usually refers to the mother's womb, but here applied to Job's own children with an unusual intimacy. These are the children born from his own body and they too require supplication.
18: gam-ʿᵉwîlîm māʾăsû bî ʾāqûmāh waydabbᵉrû-bî: 'Even young children despise me; when I stand up they speak against me.' ʿĀwîl (young child, child — sometimes child of low social standing) — even those who command no social authority openly mock Job. The phrase ʾāqûmāh waydabbᵉrû-bî ('when I rise, they speak against me') may imply that merely making a move — trying to assert some presence or dignity — provokes verbal attack from the lowest social tier. The inversion of Job's ch. 29 honor is complete: previously elders fell silent when he rose (29:9); now children jeer.
19: tāʿᵃḇûnî kāl-mᵉṯê sōḏî waʾᵃhāḇtî hāp̄ᵉḵû-bî: 'All my intimate companions abhor me; those I loved have turned against me.' Sôḏ (intimate circle, secret counsel, close associates) — the word for the innermost ring of trusted friendship (Jer 23:18; Ps 25:14; Prov 3:32; 11:13). The abhorrence (tāʿaḇ = to abhor, detest) of those in the innermost circle is the final and most devastating betrayal. ʾĀhăḇtî (those I loved) — the emotional vocabulary here is striking: Job describes these relationships with the language of love, making the turning-against an act of betrayal at the most intimate level.
20: bᵉʿôrî ûḇiḇśārî dāḇᵉqāh ʿaṣmî wāʾitmallēṭāh bᵉʿôr šinnāy: 'My bones cling to my skin and my flesh; I have escaped with the skin of my teeth.' Bᵉʿôr šinnāy is a unique hapax expression — literally 'in the skin of my teeth' — meaning escaped with absolutely nothing, by the barest margin. The teeth have no skin, so the phrase describes zero margin — survival of less than the minimum. Dāḇaq (to cling, cleave) — normally used of covenant loyalty (Gen 2:24; Ruth 1:14) is here used for bones clinging to skin because the flesh has wasted away; the word of marital union is applied to the grotesque adhesion of disease.
21: ḥonnûnî ḥonnûnî ʾattem rēʿāy kî yaḏ-ʾĕlôah nāḡᵉʿāh bî: 'Have pity on me, have pity on me, O my friends, for the hand of God has struck me.' The doubled ḥonnûnî (be gracious to me, have mercy on me) is an intensification — the same verb used in prayer to God (Ps 6:2; 30:10; 31:9; 56:1) is now directed at humans. Job applies to his friends the vocabulary of petitionary prayer, underscoring both his desperation and the irony: those from whom he begs mercy are the ones most persistently adding to his suffering. Yaḏ ʾĕlôah nāḡᵉʿāh bî: 'the hand of God has touched/struck me' — nāgaʿ (to touch, smite, reach) is the verb from 1:11 and 2:5 where the Adversary predicted Job would 'curse God to his face' if God's hand touched him. Job acknowledges the touch but does not curse.
22: lāmmāh tirḏᵉp̄ûnî kēʾl ûmibᵉśārî lōʾ ṯiśbāʿû: 'Why do you pursue me like God, and are not satisfied with my flesh?' Rāḏap̄ (pursue, chase) — the word of relentless hunting. By pursuing Job 'like God' (kēʾl), Job accuses the friends of acting with divine-scale relentlessness against a human being. They are not merely critical advisers but persecutors. Mibbᵉśārî lōʾ ṯiśbāʿû: 'you are not satisfied with my flesh' — the appetite-for-flesh imagery suggests predatory consumption; the friends have devoured his honor and reputation and still want more.
23: mî-yittēn ʾēp̄ô wᵉyikkāṯᵉḇû miллāy: 'O that my words were written down!' The longing for written testimony is the legal act of creating a permanent record that cannot be denied. Yikkāṯᵉḇû (niphal = 'were written') — the passive formulation leaves open who would inscribe them. Millîm (words, discourse) — specifically legal speech, not merely conversation. Job wants an irrefutable deposition that will outlast the moment and the witnesses. The desire for permanent testimony anticipates the divine answer: God will vindicate Job (42:7-9), and the book itself becomes the permanent record Job longed for.
24: bᵉʿēṭ bārzel wᵉʿōp̄eret laʿaḏ bᵉṣûr yᵉḥōṣᵉḇûn: 'That with an iron pen and lead they were cut into the rock forever!' ʿĒṭ bārzel (iron stylus) — a sharp iron tool for carving; ʿōp̄eret (lead) poured into the cuts to make them permanent and visible. The technology of rock inscription with metal and lead filling was practiced in the ancient world to create indelible monuments. Laʿaḏ (forever, perpetually) — Job wants testimony that outlasts any human witness. The irony: Job desired his words cut permanently in rock; instead they have been preserved in the most-read book of world literature.
26: wᵉʾaḥar ʿôrî niqqᵉp̄û-zōʾt ûmibbᵉśārî ʾeḥĕzeh ʾĕlôah: 'And after my skin is thus destroyed, yet from within my flesh I shall see God.' This verse (with v.27) is one of the most textually disputed passages in the OT. The key question is whether mibbᵉśārî means 'from my flesh' (i.e., still in the body = bodily resurrection) or 'from after/away from my flesh' (i.e., as a disembodied spirit). The Hebrew preposition min is ambiguous. The verb ḥāzāh (to see, behold — especially used of prophetic vision and direct divine encounter, Exod 24:11; Ps 17:15; 63:2) adds force to the statement: not mere knowledge of God but direct visual encounter.
27: ʾăšer ʾănî ʾeḥĕzeh-lî wᵉʿênay rāʾû wᵉlōʾ-zār kālû ḵilᵉyōṯay bᵉḥēqî: 'Whom I myself shall see, and my own eyes shall behold — not a stranger's eyes. My heart fails within me with longing.' The emphatic personal pronouns: ʾănî (I myself), ʿênay (my own eyes), wᵉlōʾ-zār (not a stranger's, not another's) — Job insists on personal, individual, direct encounter, not a third-party report. Kālû ḵilᵉyōṯay bᵉḥēqî: 'my kidneys (kilyôt) fail within my bosom with longing' — the kidneys were understood as the seat of the deepest emotions and will (Ps 7:9; 26:2; Jer 11:20); their 'failing' (consuming away) describes an intensity of longing that is physically felt.
28: kî tōʾmᵉrû maddûaʿ nirḏāp̄-lô wᵉšōreš dāḇār nimṣāʾ-bî: 'If you say, “Why are we still pursuing him?” when the root of the trouble lies within him.' Job addresses a hypothetical future conversation the friends might have. Šōreš dāḇār (root of the matter/word) — the friends assume the root of Job's problem is internal sin; Job knows the root of the matter is the divine action described in vv.6-12. The word šōreš (root) is taken up by God himself in 42:7-8, reversing the friends' diagnosis: the root of the problem was their incorrect speech about God, not Job's concealed sin.
29: gûrû lāḵem mippᵉnê-ḥereb kî-ḥēmāh ʿăwōnôt ḥāreb lᵉmaʿan tēḏᵉʿûn šaddāy: 'Fear the sword, for wrath brings the sword's punishment, so that you may know there is indeed a judgment.' Job's warning is a reversal of the retribution principle the friends have been applying to him: the sword does not threaten Job but the friends, whose wrath-driven pursuit constitutes the actual sin requiring judgment. Šaddāy — ʾēl šaddāy (God Almighty) is the divine name used almost exclusively in the book of Job (31 of the 48 total OT occurrences), emphasizing the overwhelming sovereign power of God before whom all judgment ultimately stands.
